dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a 26-year-old male reporting chest and abdominal pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Um, I've been having chest and stomach pain for hours and throwing up everything I eat or drink.
What is your name, can you tell me?
My name is... uh, Jamel.
Where are we now, Jamel?
[CITY]. I want to go to the hospital. (He moves his arms slightly)
You're responding to questions and moving your arms. You're alert to person, place.
He is oriented to time and place. An A for alert on AVPU.
Tell me, is anything making it harder to breathe or feel air going in your lungs?
It's shallow, and I get really short of breath quickly. I feel like I'm breathing too fast.
Respiratory rate is elevated - 32 initially with shallow breaths. He's tachypneic.
Partner, grab the BP and pulse while I look at skin color and temperature.
BP is 171/82, pulse is 120 - elevated. Skin is pale but warm and dry with no cyanosis.
How long has the pain been there? Was it all at once or gradual?
It just came slowly, like 3 hours ago. I have diabetes.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What were you doing when it started?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was just laying in the grass, but this started the night before.
Is there anything else we should know about your medical history?
I take insulin - it's written down but no allergies.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how bad is the pain?
It's a 7 out of 10 mostly in my chest and stomach, doesn't radiate much.
Pulse remains high - 120 beats per minute.
Respirations at 32, so definitely tachypneic.
Blood pressure is 171/82. Good perfusion but elevated heart rate.
Oxygen saturation at 99%. Doing fairly well with oxygen.
Glucose results are high at 303.
EKG is showing sinus tachycardia. There's no acute issue on the 12-lead.
IV line started in the left arm. Letting us give fluids normally.
I've just administered 300 mL bolus of normal saline.
Your skin stays pale but warm and dry with no rashes or bruising.
Head and neck are free of trauma. Pupils are equal, round, and reactive.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Any pain or obstruction in ears, nose, or throat?

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Patient
dialogue.utterance: No, that's all throat pain just from extending when I vomit.
Lungs sound clear with no wheezing or asymmetry. Chest moves symmetrically.
Peripheral pulses strong in all extremities. No deformities or swelling.
Repeat BP at 171/82 - stable but still high.
I'm going to check for signs of JVD or distended neck veins.
Nothing observed. No JVD. Neck is supple and normal.
We are following the medical nausea/vomiting protocol (protocol 3-9).
Rechecked vitals - BP down to 138/69, pulse at 116, and respiration now at 36.
Oxygen remains better at 100% with no trended down change. Glucose still 303.
IV is flowing at KVO now and patient is tolerating it well.
On a 0 to 10 scale, how would you rate your pain now, Jamel?
Still about a 7. Nothing better or worse.
Still tight and localized pain in the chest and stomach, right?
Yeah, it doesn't move and vomiting helps a little.
Jamel was alert and oriented to person and place - AOX3.
Pupils equal, round, reactive, and skin remains pale with no rashes, bruising noted.
Per EKG, it's clear of any acute findings besides sinus tachycardia. Stable.
Blood pressure dropped slightly to 138/69, heart rate reduced to 116, and respiratory rate increased to 36.
Blood glucose remained high at 303 throughout transport. No change.
We administered 300 mL of normal saline via IV per protocol. IV remains patent.
Patient is an insulin-dependent diabetic with no known drug allergies.
Mention to ER that medication he was taking prior was Humalog.
No neurological abnormalities. No head or neck trauma noted.
Patient reported using the trash can for vomiting - no fluid retention or difficulty keeping food down.
No signs of shock or instability. No abdominal rigidity or guarding.
Let ER team confirm his last insulin time and fluid intake before turnout.
No new signs during transport - he remained oriented with no change in mental status.
Transported securely with all straps in place. Movement uneventful.
No procedure issues. Emergency care protocols applied properly.
Transporting him to [COMPANY_NAME] per hospital sector assignment.
He's buckled in securely. Let's adjust his position for transport comfort.
Moving him into the ambulance now - no patient movement required.
Using [CITY]'s transport system for smoother travel.
You're in good hands. We just need to take you to the hospital now.
Prep the stretcher so he can transition smoothly to the ER bed once we arrive.
ER team has full report for vitals, intervention, and patient medical status.
Let the charge nurse know his transport was stable with no complaint during ride.
Patient accepted by [CITY] staff with all documentation on standby.
No emergency override required - he walked into ER bed under his own power.
All documentation locked and transferred via EPCR. Inform ER of his timeline and fluid status.
Legal signature provided himself. No guardians or family immediately present.
We'll be available if ER needs any clarification or more info during assessment.
Handing over EMS team details now. Ready for hospital transition.
Patient moving smoothly into the ER - no transport incident to report.
Update the ER on final vitals before handover.
Noted he tolerated all transport positions and no pain spike reported.
All straps removed. Ambulance ready for next call.
